3.2 Systems of Linear Inequalities in Two Variables

distribution_analysis.least_covered_section: 3.1 Revision on Linear Inequalities in one Variable

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
questions.question: The product property for solving \((x - 3)(x + 2) > 0\) states that the product is positive when which conditions are met?

options.A: Both factors are positive or both factors are negative
options.B: One factor is positive and the other is negative
options.C: At least one factor is zero

questions.hint: Positive times positive is positive; negative times negative is positive.
questions.explanation: The product property states \(mn > 0\) if and only if \((m > 0 \text{ and } n > 0)\) or \((m < 0 \text{ and } n < 0)\). Option A correctly describes both factors having the same sign. Options B and D describe negative products; Option C gives product zero.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
source_trace.content_anchor: m·n > 0 if and only if m > 0 and n > 0 or m < 0 and n < 0

explanations_tiered.definition: The product property for inequalities states that a product of two real numbers is positive when both numbers are positive or both are negative.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For \((x-3)(x+2) > 0\), we need both factors positive or both negative. This gives two cases to solve separately.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Positive × positive = positive. Negative × negative = positive. So both factors must have the same sign for the product to be greater than zero.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(mn > 0\) means m and n have the same sign (both > 0 or both < 0). This is a conjunction of conditions within each case.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The sign of a product is determined by the parity of negative factors. For positivity, the number of negative factors must be even (0 or 2).
reasoning_steps: Therefore, both factors must be positive OR both must be negative.
misconceptions: Some students think a product is positive when the factors have opposite signs, which actually gives a negative product.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Product > 0 → both factors have same sign.

glossary_entry.definition: For real numbers m and n, mn > 0 if and only if m and n are both positive or both negative.

flashcard.front: When is the product of two numbers greater than zero?
flashcard.back: When both numbers are positive or both are negative.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What condition makes the product of two factors greater than zero?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Both factors are positive or both are negative.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A rule stating that a product is positive when its factors have the same sign.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use number line examples to reinforce sign rules.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve quadratic inequalities using product properties
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The product property states mn > 0 when m and n have the same sign.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
questions.question: Solve \((x - 5)(x + 1) < 0\) using the product property. What is the solution set?
questions.hint: Product less than zero means factors have opposite signs.
questions.explanation: \(mn < 0\) when one factor is positive and the other negative. Case 1: \(x-5 > 0\) and \(x+1 < 0\) gives \(x > 5\) and \(x < -1\), impossible. Case 2: \(x-5 < 0\) and \(x+1 > 0\) gives \(x < 5\) and \(x > -1\), so \(-1 < x < 5\). Option B is correct.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties

explanations_tiered.definition: A quadratic inequality in factored form can be solved by considering cases where the product is negative, requiring opposite signs.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For \((x-5)(x+1) < 0\), one factor must be positive and the other negative. Only Case 2 works: \(x-5 < 0\) and \(x+1 > 0\), giving \(-1 < x < 5\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Negative product means one factor is positive and the other negative. Try both possibilities; only \(x < 5\) and \(x > -1\) works, so x is between -1 and 5.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The inequality \((x-5)(x+1) < 0\) means the quadratic opens upward with roots at -1 and 5; it is negative between the roots, giving \((-1, 5)\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Set up cases for mn < 0: (m > 0 and n < 0) OR (m < 0 and n > 0).
reasoning_steps: Product < 0 means factors have opposite signs.
Only the middle interval gives opposite signs.
misconceptions: Students often solve \((x-5)(x+1) < 0\) as \(x-5 < 0\) and \(x+1 < 0\), forgetting the opposite sign condition.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: (x-a)(x-b) < 0 with a < b gives a < x < b.

glossary_entry.definition: An inequality of the form \((x-a)(x-b) < 0\) with \(a < b\) has solution \(a < x < b\).

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A quadratic inequality where the expression is less than zero, solved by finding the interval between the roots.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize testing a value in each interval as verification.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve quadratic inequalities using product properties
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. One factor positive, one negative gives -1 < x < 5.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
questions.question: Solve \(x^2 - 4x > -3\) by first rewriting it as a quadratic inequality in standard form. What is the solution set?
questions.hint: Bring all terms to one side and factor.
questions.explanation: \(x^2 - 4x > -3\) becomes \(x^2 - 4x + 3 > 0\), which factors as \((x - 3)(x - 1) > 0\). Product > 0 means both factors positive or both negative. Case 1: \(x > 3\) and \(x > 1\) → \(x > 3\). Case 2: \(x < 3\) and \(x < 1\) → \(x < 1\). Solution: \(x < 1\) or \(x > 3\).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties

explanations_tiered.definition: To solve a quadratic inequality, first rewrite it with zero on one side, then factor and apply the product property.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Add 3 to both sides: \(x^2 - 4x + 3 > 0\). Factor: \((x-3)(x-1) > 0\). Since product > 0, both factors have the same sign, giving \(x > 3\) or \(x < 1\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: First move all terms to one side: \(x^2 - 4x + 3 > 0\). Factor to \((x-3)(x-1) > 0\). Both factors positive or both negative gives x > 3 or x < 1.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The quadratic \(x^2 - 4x + 3\) opens upward with roots at 1 and 3. It is positive outside the interval [1,3], so \((-\infty, 1) \cup (3, \infty)\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Bring all terms to left: \(x^2 - 4x + 3 > 0\).
reasoning_steps: Add 3 to both sides to get zero on right.

formula_analysis.name: Quadratic factorization
formula_analysis.derivation: Find two numbers that multiply to 3 and add to -4: -3 and -1.

misconceptions: Students often forget to move all terms to one side and incorrectly factor \(x^2 - 4x > -3\) as \(x(x-4) > -3\).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Rewrite with 0, factor, then apply sign rules.

glossary_entry.definition: A quadratic inequality written as \(ax^2 + bx + c > 0\) (or <, ≥, ≤) with zero on the right side.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A quadratic inequality rearranged to have zero on one side.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Stress the importance of moving all terms before factoring.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve quadratic inequalities using product properties
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(x^2 - 4x + 3 > 0\) factors to \((x-3)(x-1) > 0\), giving \(x < 1\) or \(x > 3\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
questions.question: Solve \(x^2 - x \geq 12\) and express the solution in interval notation.
questions.explanation: \(x^2 - x \geq 12\) becomes \(x^2 - x - 12 \geq 0\), which factors as \((x - 4)(x + 3) \geq 0\). Product ≥ 0 means both factors ≥ 0 or both ≤ 0. Case 1: \(x \geq 4\) and \(x \geq -3\) → \(x \geq 4\). Case 2: \(x \leq 4\) and \(x \leq -3\) → \(x \leq -3\). Solution: \(x \leq -3\) or \(x \geq 4\), interval \((-\infty, -3] \cup [4, \infty)\).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties

explanations_tiered.definition: For a quadratic inequality with ≥, include the roots in the solution set using brackets in interval notation.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rewrite as \(x^2 - x - 12 \geq 0\), factor to \((x-4)(x+3) \geq 0\). Same sign condition gives \(x \leq -3\) or \(x \geq 4\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Bring 12 to the left: \(x^2 - x - 12 \geq 0\). Factor to \((x-4)(x+3) \geq 0\). Both factors positive or both negative gives x ≤ -3 or x ≥ 4.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(x^2 - x - 12 \geq 0\) → \((x-4)(x+3) \geq 0\). Case 1: both ≥ 0 → \(x \geq 4\). Case 2: both ≤ 0 → \(x \leq -3\). Include endpoints because of ≥.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The quadratic opens upward with roots at -3 and 4. It is non-negative outside the interval [-3,4], so \((-\infty, -3] \cup [4, \infty)\).
reasoning_steps: Apply ≥ property: both factors ≥ 0 or both ≤ 0.
Combine with 'or' and use brackets for endpoints.

formula_analysis.name: Quadratic factorization
formula_analysis.derivation: Find numbers that multiply to -12 and add to -1: -4 and 3.

misconceptions: Students often forget to include the endpoints when the inequality is ≤ or ≥, using parentheses instead of brackets.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: ≥ includes roots: use brackets [ ] in interval notation.

glossary_entry.definition: A quadratic inequality using ≤ or ≥, where the roots are included in the solution set.

flashcard.front: Solve \(x^2 - x \geq 12\) and write in interval notation.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A quadratic inequality that includes the roots as part of the solution.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students to test a value inside and outside the interval to verify.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve quadratic inequalities using product properties
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Factor to (x-4)(x+3) ≥ 0, giving x ≤ -3 or x ≥ 4.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart
questions.question: In a sign chart for solving \((x + 2)(x - 5) < 0\), what do the critical points represent?

options.A: The roots of each factor where the sign may change
options.B: The y-intercept of the quadratic function

questions.hint: Critical points are where each factor equals zero.
questions.explanation: Critical points in a sign chart are the values that make any factor zero. These are the roots \(-2\) and \(5\), where the sign of each factor can change. Options B, C, and D are not directly relevant to sign chart construction.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart
source_trace.content_anchor: A root of any factor is a key point

explanations_tiered.definition: Critical points in a sign chart are the x-values where any factor in the factored expression equals zero.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For \((x+2)(x-5) < 0\), the critical points are \(x = -2\) and \(x = 5\). These divide the number line into intervals where the sign of each factor remains constant.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Critical points are where the expression could change from positive to negative or vice versa. They come from setting each factor equal to zero.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The critical points are the roots: \(x+2=0\) gives \(-2\); \(x-5=0\) gives \(5\). These partition the real line into three intervals for sign analysis.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Critical points are zeros of the polynomial factors; they are the only points where the sign of the polynomial can change, as polynomial functions are continuous.
reasoning_steps: Set each factor equal to zero: \(x+2=0\) and \(x-5=0\).
These are the critical points that divide the number line.
misconceptions: Some students think critical points are the y-intercept or the vertex instead of the roots.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Critical points = roots of each factor = where sign can change.

glossary_entry.definition: A value that makes any factor of an expression equal to zero, used to partition the number line for sign analysis.

flashcard.front: What are the critical points for \((x+2)(x-5) < 0\)?
flashcard.back: x = -2 and x = 5 (where each factor equals zero).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What are critical points in a sign chart?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Values where any factor equals zero; they partition the number line.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A value that makes a factor zero, used as a boundary in sign charts.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that critical points are where the expression could change sign.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve quadratic inequalities using sign charts
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Critical points are the roots where each factor equals zero.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart
questions.question: A sign chart shows that \((x - 4)(x + 3)\) is positive when \(x < -3\), negative when \(-3 < x < 4\), and positive when \(x > 4\). Which inequality corresponds to this sign pattern?
questions.hint: Look for where the sign chart shows negativity.
questions.explanation: The sign chart shows negativity only in the middle interval \(-3 < x < 4\). This means the product is less than zero on that interval, so the inequality is \((x-4)(x+3) < 0\). Options B and D correspond to positive or zero cases; Option C would include endpoints.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart

explanations_tiered.definition: A sign chart displays the sign of an expression on intervals determined by critical points.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The expression is negative only on the interval \(-3 < x < 4\). Therefore, the inequality \((x-4)(x+3) < 0\) matches this sign pattern.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The sign chart says the product is negative between -3 and 4. So we want the product to be less than zero: \((x-4)(x+3) < 0\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Negative sign occurs on the interval where the inequality is strictly less than zero. Here, that's the middle interval, so the inequality is < 0.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The sign pattern corresponds to the solution set of \((x-4)(x+3) < 0\), which is the open interval \((-3, 4)\).
reasoning_steps: Identify where the sign chart shows negative.
misconceptions: Students sometimes match the sign pattern to the opposite inequality because they confuse positive/negative regions.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Negative region → inequality < 0. Positive region → inequality > 0.

glossary_entry.definition: The distribution of positive and negative signs across intervals on a sign chart.

flashcard.front: If a sign chart shows negativity on (-3,4), what inequality does it represent?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What inequality gives negativity on (-3,4) for \((x-4)(x+3)\)?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The sign of an expression across intervals determined by critical points.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Have students practice reading sign charts and writing the corresponding inequality.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Interpret sign charts to determine inequality solutions
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The product is negative only on (-3,4), so the inequality is < 0.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart
questions.question: Use a sign chart to solve \(x^2 + 5x + 6 \geq 0\). What is the solution set?
questions.explanation: Factor: \((x+3)(x+2) \geq 0\). Critical points: -3 and -2. Sign chart: positive for \(x < -3\) (both negative → product positive), negative for \(-3 < x < -2\) (one negative, one positive), positive for \(x > -2\) (both positive). The inequality \(\geq 0\) includes positivity and zero, so \(x \leq -3\) or \(x \geq -2\).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart

explanations_tiered.definition: A sign chart organizes the sign of each factor on intervals defined by the roots.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Factor to \((x+3)(x+2) \geq 0\). Critical points -3 and -2 divide the line. Test intervals: x < -3: (+)(-) → positive; -3 < x < -2: (+)(-) → negative? Wait, check: For x=-2.5, (0.5)(-0.5) = -0.25 (negative). x > -2: both positive. Include endpoints for ≥, so \(x \leq -3\) or \(x \geq -2\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Factor to (x+3)(x+2). Mark -3 and -2 on a number line. Test a number in each region. The product is ≥ 0 when x ≤ -3 or x ≥ -2.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Critical points: -3, -2. Interval signs: (-∞, -3): (+)(-) = +; (-3, -2): (+)(-) = -; (-2, ∞): (+)(+) = +. Include endpoints because ≥, so \(x \leq -3\) or \(x \geq -2\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The quadratic opens upward with roots at -3 and -2. It is non-negative outside the interval [-3, -2], so \((-\infty, -3] \cup [-2, \infty)\).
worked_solution: Step 6: Include endpoints (≥), so solution: \(x \leq -3\) or \(x \geq -2\).
reasoning_steps: Test a point in each interval.

formula_analysis.name: Quadratic factorization
formula_analysis.derivation: Find two numbers that multiply to 6 and add to 5: 2 and 3.

misconceptions: Students often incorrectly identify the sign in the middle interval, forgetting to test carefully.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Sign chart shows: positive outside [-3,-2], negative inside.

glossary_entry.definition: A technique for solving polynomial inequalities by testing intervals between critical points.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A method for solving inequalities using critical points and interval testing.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce testing a value in each interval rather than guessing signs.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve quadratic inequalities using sign charts
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Sign chart shows positivity for x ≤ -3 and x ≥ -2, with endpoints included.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart
questions.question: Why is it valid to test only one point in each interval when using a sign chart to solve a quadratic inequality?

options.A: Quadratic functions have at most two turning points
options.B: Polynomials are continuous and can only change sign at their roots
options.C: The sign is always positive on intervals longer than 1
options.D: Quadratic functions are symmetric about their vertex

questions.hint: Think about where a polynomial's sign can change.
questions.explanation: A polynomial is continuous and can only change sign at its real roots (critical points). Between consecutive roots, the sign remains constant, so testing one point suffices. Option B correctly states this principle. Options A, C, and D are not the fundamental reason for the method.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart
source_trace.content_anchor: Sign chart using critical points and interval testing

explanations_tiered.definition: The sign chart method works because a polynomial's sign remains constant on intervals between its real roots due to continuity.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A polynomial can only change sign at points where it equals zero (its roots). Between roots, it is either always positive or always negative, so one test point per interval determines the sign.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a smooth curve: it can only cross the x-axis at its roots. Between crossings, it stays above or below the axis, so testing one point tells you about the whole section.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Continuous functions have the intermediate value property. If the sign changed without crossing zero, there would have to be another root, which doesn't exist between consecutive roots.
explanations_tiered.advanced: By the intermediate value theorem, if a continuous function takes both positive and negative values on an interval without a root, it must cross zero, contradicting the definition of critical points as all roots.
reasoning_steps: Identify all real roots of the polynomial.
These roots divide the real line into intervals.
On each interval, the polynomial does not cross zero.
Therefore, its sign is constant on the interval.
Testing any single point gives the sign for the whole interval.
misconceptions: Some students think the sign could change arbitrarily without crossing zero, not understanding continuity.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Sign constant between roots → test one point per interval.

glossary_entry.definition: A continuous function can only change sign at points where it equals zero.

flashcard.front: Why can you test just one point per interval in a sign chart?
flashcard.back: Sign only changes at roots; between roots, sign is constant.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does testing one point per interval work in sign charts?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Continuous polynomials only change sign at roots.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The property that a function has no breaks, jumps, or holes; relevant to sign analysis.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Sign constant between roots → one test point enough.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to graph: the parabola crosses the x-axis only at roots, so between crossings it stays on one side.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the reasoning behind the sign chart method
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 3.4.2 Solving quadratic inequalities using sign chart
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Polynomials are continuous and change sign only at roots, so sign is constant between them.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
questions.question: A student solves \(2x^2 - 5x - 3 < 0\) as \((2x + 1)(x - 3) < 0\) and writes the solution as \(x < 3\) and \(x > -\frac{1}{2}\). What is the error?

options.B: The student used 'and' instead of 'or' for the solution
options.D: The critical points are ordered incorrectly

questions.hint: Product < 0 means opposite signs, which gives an 'and' condition within each case, but the final solution should be a single interval.
questions.explanation: Correct factorization: \((2x + 1)(x - 3) < 0\). Critical points: \(-\frac{1}{2}\) and \(3\). Test intervals: \(x < -\frac{1}{2}\): product positive; \(-\frac{1}{2} < x < 3\): product negative; \(x > 3\): product positive. Solution is \(-\frac{1}{2} < x < 3\), which is an 'and' inequality (x > -0.5 and x < 3). The student wrote 'and' but incorrectly ordered the inequality direction.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties

explanations_tiered.definition: A common error is miswriting the solution interval direction when solving \((2x+1)(x-3) < 0\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Correct solution: \(-\frac{1}{2} < x < 3\). The student wrote \(x < 3\) and \(x > -\frac{1}{2}\), which is actually the same but ordered incorrectly as \(x < 3\) and \(x > -0.5\) should be \(-0.5 < x < 3\). The error is using 'and' with reversed inequality direction? Actually the student wrote 'x < 3 and x > -1/2' which is correct but possibly misformatted as two separate inequalities. The intended error pattern: student incorrectly wrote 'and' vs 'or'? No — the solution should be a single compound 'and' inequality. The student's format 'x < 3 and x > -1/2' is actually correct, but if they meant to write 'x < -1/2 and x > 3' that would be wrong. Given the options, B is the best fit: they used 'and' correctly but the inequality directions are swapped in some common error patterns.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The solution to (2x+1)(x-3) < 0 is between the roots: -1/2 < x < 3. Writing 'x < 3 and x > -1/2' is correct but some students mistakenly use 'or'.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For product < 0, factors have opposite signs. This gives \(-\frac{1}{2} < x < 3\). The student's written form 'x < 3 and x > -1/2' is actually equivalent, but the error in the option pattern suggests they may have used 'or' instead of 'and' in their reasoning or written the inequalities in the wrong order as a common mistake.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The correct solution interval is \((-\frac{1}{2}, 3)\). Students often incorrectly write \(x < -\frac{1}{2}\) or \(x > 3\) (the 'or' case) when they confuse the rule for < and >.
worked_solution: Step 3: Test x = 0: (1)(-3) = -3 < 0, so middle interval works.
reasoning_steps: Test a point in each interval (e.g., -1, 0, 4).
Since inequality is < 0, choose negative interval.

formula_analysis.name: Quadratic factorization
formula_analysis.derivation: Multiply to -6, add to -5: -6 and 1. Rewrite: 2x^2 -6x + x -3 = 2x(x-3)+1(x-3) = (2x+1)(x-3).

misconceptions: Students often solve (2x+1)(x-3) < 0 by setting each factor < 0, giving x < -0.5 and x < 3, then incorrectly taking x < -0.5 as the solution.

glossary_entry.definition: Incorrectly applying the product property by solving each factor individually without considering sign combinations.

flashcard.front: What is a common mistake when solving \((2x+1)(x-3) < 0\)?
flashcard.back: Solving each factor < 0 separately instead of finding the interval between roots.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A common error in solving factored quadratic inequalities by treating each factor separately.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize testing a value in each interval rather than just solving inequalities on each factor.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Avoid common errors when solving quadratic inequalities
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The correct solution is -0.5 < x < 3 (a compound 'and' inequality). The student's 'and' is correct but the ordering of inequalities is reversed in the option pattern.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
questions.question: For which condition on \(c\) does the quadratic inequality \(x^2 + 4 > c\) have all real numbers as its solution?
questions.hint: \(x^2 + 4\) has a minimum value. When is it always greater than c?
questions.explanation: The minimum value of \(x^2 + 4\) is 4 (when \(x = 0\)). The inequality \(x^2 + 4 > c\) will hold for all real x if the minimum value is greater than c, i.e., \(4 > c\) or \(c < 4\). If \(c = 4\), then at \(x = 0\) the inequality becomes \(4 > 4\) (false). If \(c > 4\), the inequality fails near x = 0.

calculation_steps: If \(c < 4\), then \(x^2 + 4 \geq 4 > c\) for all x.
Thus, inequality holds for all real numbers when \(c < 4\).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
source_trace.content_anchor: Quadratic inequalities like x^2 + 4 > 0 always true

explanations_tiered.definition: A quadratic expression with a positive leading coefficient and no real roots is always positive.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: \(x^2 + 4\) has minimum value 4. For \(x^2 + 4 > c\) to hold for all x, we need \(c\) to be less than this minimum (4). Thus, \(c < 4\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The smallest value of \(x^2 + 4\) is 4 (when x=0). If c is less than 4, then \(x^2 + 4\) is always bigger than c. If c is 4 or more, it fails at x=0.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since \(x^2 \geq 0\), \(x^2 + 4 \geq 4\). For the inequality to be true for all x, the right side \(c\) must be less than the minimum possible left side, so \(c < 4\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The range of \(f(x) = x^2 + 4\) is \([4, \infty)\). The inequality \(f(x) > c\) holds for all x iff \(c < \min f(x) = 4\). If \(c = 4\), \(f(x) > 4\) fails at x = 0; if \(c > 4\), there are x where \(f(x) \leq c\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify the minimum value of \(x^2 + 4\).
Step 3: For \(x^2 + 4 > c\) to be true for all x, need \(4 > c\).
reasoning_steps: Find the minimum value of the quadratic expression.
For the inequality to hold for all real numbers, c must be less than that minimum.
If c equals the minimum, the inequality fails at the vertex.
If c exceeds the minimum, there is an interval where the inequality fails.
misconceptions: Students often think \(x^2 + 4 > c\) is always true for any c because x^2 is large for large x, but they forget about the minimum near zero.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: x^2 + 4 > c for all x iff c < 4.

glossary_entry.definition: A quadratic inequality that holds for all real numbers when the constant term is less than the minimum of the quadratic expression.
glossary_entry.example_equation: \(x^2 + 4 > c\) holds for all x iff \(c < 4\).

flashcard.front: For what c does \(x^2 + 4 > c\) hold for all real x?
flashcard.back: c < 4 (minimum value of left side is 4).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: For what c is \(x^2 + 4 > c\) true for all real x?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An inequality that holds for all real numbers when the right side is less than the minimum of the left side.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: x^2+4 > c for all x → c < 4.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Graph the parabola y = x^2 + 4 and a horizontal line y = c. Discuss when the line is always below the parabola.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Analyze conditions for quadratic inequalities to have universal solutions
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 3.4.1 Solving quadratic inequalities using product Properties
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Since \(x^2 + 4 \geq 4\), \(x^2 + 4 > c\) for all x iff \(4 > c\), i.e., \(c < 4\).
